evidence_quotes.quote: These marketplaces typically use a fictional currency that makes it harder to gauge the actual price of goods.

paragraphs.text: Digital goods are often restricted in multiple ways. Often the creator of these goods can change how those restrictions are applied even after the sale. In practice this means that digital goods can stop functioning from one day to the other. This can happen for multiple reasons (software bugs preventing a connection to the cloud service, internet outages, corporations going bankrupt or even business decisions). Examples for this can be found in video games (for which I assume other comments have already pointed out many such cases), but not exclusively. E-Books and other digital media are often only usable with specific reading devices that support the DRM scheme. Copies are often bound to a specific device. If the DRM fails or servers are shut down for it, then there will be no legal way to transfer those goods to another device. Even physical goods can suffer from this, if they are programmed to only work with a specific cloud service. A discontinued cloud service can immediately render thousands of devices paperweights. One step further are marketplaces where one can purchase digital goods, but the goods can only be accessed through that marketplace. Examples here are comic sites like Tapas, Webtoons, or Bilibili. Though one can argue that video games with purchasable constant (microtransactions) fall in the same category. These marketplaces typically use a fictional currency that makes it harder to gauge the actual price of goods. Once the marketplace shuts down (as happened with many video games and the mentioned Bilibili [only the English part was shutdown here]) customers cannot access their purchased goods anymore. The customer usually has no viable defense against the sudden loss of value for these digital goods. Even if they are technically able to repair the digital good by updating part of the machine code, e.g., to patch out calls to a discontinued cloud service, they risk breaking the law doing so. To repair code one first has to defeat the restrictions placed by the creator, which equates to circumventing a piracy prevention mechanism (violating copyright laws or anti-hacking laws). Thus, these digital restrictions greatly favor the creator of digital goods and run in conflict of the expected consumer rights. This situation in part enables some of the dark patterns that this legislation wants to combat. In order for the consumer rights that this legislation wants to establish to be effective, there should be some viable defense for customers against the root cause the digital restrictions. Allowing the customers to circumvent these restrictions (and more importantly share the circumvention with other affected customers!) for the purpose of enforcing their rights could be an effective solution. If this will not be allowed, customers should at least have effective means to force the publisher of digital goods to honor their customer rights, even if that means that the publisher has to be forced to adjust those digital restrictions himself. The digital restrictions also prevent selling digital goods as used goods to other consumers. This practice is possible with physical goods and can allow consumers with less available income to still purchase those used goods. This is another way that customers can get the short end of the stick with digital goods.
